intro.value: This transition is the discrete-to-continuum bridge for the order parameter. Eq. 4 is still a finite-N empirical measure over phase, while Eq. 5 is the continuum average over phase and natural frequency.
The main new idea is to replace the delta-comb of actual oscillators by a smooth density rho of theta, omega, and t, weighted by the frequency distribution g of omega.

steps.rightTitle: Eq. 4 as an Empirical Phase Density

left.value: Eq. 4 already rewrites the finite sum from Eq. 2 as an integral. But the integrand is still only a comb of delta spikes at the observed oscillator phases.
So this is not yet a continuum theory. It is still the finite population written in measure form.

steps.rightTitle: Refine the Description by Natural Frequency

left.value: To take the continuum limit, we do not only track where oscillators are on the phase circle. We also track which natural frequency class they belong to.
That is why the continuum description uses a joint density: rho of theta, omega, t for phase at fixed omega, together with g of omega for how common each natural frequency is in the population.

steps.rightTitle: Replace the Empirical Average by a Continuum Average

left.value: Once the empirical measure is replaced by the continuum density, the phase average becomes a double integral over theta and omega.
This is the same order parameter idea as before, but now expressed for an infinite population described by a density.
